text: These are the names of the children of Israel, that went into Egypt with Jacob: they went in every man with his household:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Ruben, Simeon, Levi, Juda,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Issachar, Zabulon, and Benjamin,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Dan, and Nephthali, Gad and Aser.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the souls that came out of Jacob’s thigh, were seventy: but Joseph was in Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After he was dead, and all his brethren, and all that generation,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The children of Israel increased, and sprung up into multitudes, and growing exceedingly strong they filled the land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the mean time there arose a new king over Egypt, that knew not Joseph:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to his people: Behold the people of the children of Israel are numerous and stronger than we.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Come let us wisely oppress them, lest they multiply: and if any war shall rise against us, join with our enemies, and having overcome us, depart out of the land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore he set over them masters of the works, to afflict them with burdens: and they built for Pharao cities of tabernacles, Phithom, and Ramesses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the more they oppressed them, the more they were multiplied and increased.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Egyptians hated the children of Israel, and afflicted them and mocked them:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they made their life bitter with hard works in clay and brick, and with all manner of service, wherewith they were overcharged in the works of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king of Egypt spoke to the midwives of the Hebrews: of whom one was called Sephora, the other Phua,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Commanding them: When you shall do the office of midwives to the Hebrew women, and the time of delivery is come: if it be a man child, kill it: if a woman, keep it alive.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the midwives feared God, and did not do as the king of Egypt had commanded, but saved the men children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king called for them and said: What is it that you meant to do, that you would save the men children?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They answered: The Hebrew women are not as the Egyptian women: for they themselves are skilful in the office of a midwife; and they are delivered before we come to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore God dealt well with the midwives: and the people multiplied and grew exceedingly strong.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And because the midwives feared God, he built them houses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Pharao therefore charged all his people, saying: Whatsoever shall be born of the male sex, ye shall cast into the river: whatsoever of the female, ye shall save alive.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After this there went a man of the house of Levi; and took a wife of his own kindred.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she conceived, and bore a son: and seeing him a goodly child, hid him three months.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when she could hide him no longer, she took a basket made of bulrushes, and daubed it with slime and pitch: and put the little babe therein, and laid him in the sedges by the river’s brink,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His sister standing afar off, and taking notice what would be done.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold the daughter of Pharao came down to wash herself in the river: and her maids walked by the river’s brink. And when she saw the basket in the sedges she sent one of her maids for it: and when it was brought,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She opened it, and seeing within it an infant crying, having compassion on it, she said: This is one of the babes of the Hebrews.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the child’s sister said to her: Shall I go, and call to thee a Hebrew woman, to nurse the babe?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She answered: Go. The maid went and called her mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao’s daughter said to her: Take this child, and nurse him for me: I will give thee thy wages. The woman took and nursed the child: and when he was grown up, she delivered him to Pharao’s daughter.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she adopted him for a son, and called him Moses, saying: Because I took him out of the water.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In those days, after Moses was grown up, he went out to his brethren: and saw their affliction, and an Egyptian striking one of the Hebrews, his brethren.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had looked about this way and that way, and saw no one there, he slew the Egyptian and hid him in the sand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And going out the next day, he saw two Hebrews quarrelling: and he said to him that did the wrong: Why strikest thou thy neighbour?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he answered: Who hath appointed thee prince and judge over us? wilt thou kill me, as thou didst yesterday kill the Egyptian? Moses feared, and said: How is this come to be known?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao heard of this word, and sought to kill Moses: but he fled from his sight, and abode in the land of Madian, and he sat down by a well.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the priest of Madian had seven daughters, who came to draw water: and when the troughs were filled, desired to water their father’s flocks.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the shepherds came and drove them away: and Moses arose, and defending the maids, watered their sheep.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they returned to Raguel their father, he said to them: Why are ye come sooner than usual?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They answered: A man of Egypt delivered us from the hands of the shepherds: and he drew water also with us, and gave the sheep to drink.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he said: Where is he? why have you let the man go? call him that he may eat bread.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses swore that he would dwell with him. And he took Sephora his daughter to wife:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she bore him a son, whom he called Gersam, saying: I have been a stranger in a foreign country. And she bore another, whom he called Eliezer, saying: For the God of my father, my helper, hath delivered me out of the hand of Pharao.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now after a long time the king of Egypt died: and the children of Israel groaning, cried out because of the works: and their cry went up unto God from the works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he heard their groaning, and remembered the covenant which he made with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord looked upon the children of Israel, and he knew them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Moses fed the sheep of Jethro, his father in law, the priest of Madian: and he drove the flock to the inner parts of the desert, and came to the mountain of God, Horeb.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord appeared to him in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush: and he saw that the bush was on fire, and was not burnt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses said: I will go, and see this great sight, why the bush is not burnt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the Lord saw that he went forward to see, he called to him out of the midst of the bush. and said: Moses, Moses. And he answered: Here I am.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: Come not nigh hither, put off the shoes from thy feet; for the place, whereon thou standest, is holy ground.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: I am the God of thy father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob. Moses hid his face: for he durst not look at God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to him: I have seen the affliction of my people in Egypt, and I have heard their cry because of the rigour of them that are over the works;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And knowing their sorrow, I am come down to deliver them out of the hands of the Egyptians, and to bring them out of that land into a good and spacious land, into a land that floweth with milk and honey, to the places of the Chanaanite, and Hethite, and Amorrhite, and Pherezite, and Hevite, and Jebusite.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the cry of the children of Israel is come unto me: and I have seen their affliction, wherewith they are oppressed by the Egyptians.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But come, and I will send thee to Pharao, that thou mayst bring forth my people, the children of Israel, out of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses said to God: Who am I that I should go to Pharao, and should bring forth the children of Israel out of Egypt?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to him: I will be with thee; and this thou shalt have for a sign that I have sent thee: When thou shalt have brought my people out of Egypt, thou shalt offer sacrifice to God upon this mountain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moses said to God: Lo, I shall go to the children of Israel, and say to them: The God of your fathers hath sent me to you. If they shall say to me: What is his name? What shall I say to them?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God said to Moses: I AM WHO AM. He said: Thus shalt thou say to the children of Israel: HE WHO IS, hath sent me to you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And God said again to Moses: Thus shalt thou say to the children of Israel: The Lord God of your fathers the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob hath sent me to you; this is my name for ever, and this is my memorial unto all generations.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go and gather together the ancients of Israel, and thou shalt say to them: The Lord God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, hath appeared to me, saying: Visiting I have visited you; and I have seen all that hath befallen you in Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I have said the word to bring you forth out of the affliction of Egypt, into the land of the Chanaanite, and Hethite, and Amorrhite, and Pherezite, and Hevite, and Jebusite, to a land that floweth with milk and honey.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they shall hear thy voice; and thou shalt go in, thou and the ancients of Israel, to the king of Egypt, and thou shalt say to him: The Lord God of the Hebrews hath called us; we will go three days’ journey into the wilderness, to sacrifice unto the Lord our God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But I know that the king of Egypt will not let you go, but by a mighty hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I will stretch forth my hand, and will strike Egypt with all my wonders which I will do in the midst of them: after these he will let you go.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I will give favour to this people, in the sight of the Egyptians: and when you go forth, you shall not depart empty:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But every woman shall ask of her neighbour, and of her that is in her house, vessels of silver and of gold, and raiment: and you shall put them on your sons and daughters, and shall spoil Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moses answered, and said: They will not believe me, nor hear my voice, but they will say: The Lord hath not appeared to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then he said to him: What is that thou holdest in thy hand? He answered: A rod.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said: Cast it down upon the ground. He cast it down, and it was turned into a serpent, so that Moses fled from it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said: Put out thy hand, and take it by the tail. He put forth his hand, and took hold of it, and it was turned into a rod.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That they may believe, saith he, that the Lord God of their fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, hath appeared to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said again: Put thy hand into thy bosom. And when he had put it into his bosom, he brought it forth leprous as snow.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: Put back thy hand into thy bosom. He put it back, and brought it out again, and it was like the other flesh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If they will not believe thee, saith he, nor hear the voice of the former sign, they will believe the word of the latter sign.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if they will not even believe these two signs, nor hear thy voice: take of the river water, and pour it out upon the dry land, and whatsoever thou drawest out of the river, shall be turned into blood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moses said: I beseech thee, Lord, I am not eloquent from yesterday and the day before; and since thou hast spoken to thy servant, I have more impediment and slowness of tongue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord said to him: Who made man’s mouth? or who made the dumb and the deaf, the seeing and the blind? did not I?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go therefore, and I will be in thy mouth; and I will teach thee what thou shalt speak.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he said: I beseech thee, Lord, send whom thou wilt send.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord being angry at Moses, said: Aaron the Levite is thy brother, I know that he is eloquent: behold he cometh forth to meet thee, and seeing thee, shall be glad at heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Speak to him, and put my words in his mouth: and I will be in thy mouth, and in his mouth, and will shew you what you must do.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall speak in thy stead to the people, and shall be thy mouth: but thou shalt be to him in those things that pertain to God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And take this rod in thy hand, wherewith thou shalt do the signs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moses went his way, and returned to Jethro his father in law, and said to him; I will go and return to my brethren into Egypt, that I may see if they be yet alive. And Jethro said to him: Go in peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses, in Madian: Go, and return into Egypt; for they are all dead that sought thy life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moses therefore took his wife, and his sons, and set them upon an ass; and returned into Egypt, carrying the rod of God in his hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to him as he was returning into Egypt: See that thou do all the wonders before Pharao, which I have put in thy hand: I shall harden his heart, and he will not let the people go.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou shalt say to him: Thus saith the Lord: Israel is my son, my firstborn.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have said to thee: Let my son go, that he may serve me, and thou wouldst not let him go: behold I will kill thy son, thy firstborn.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was in his journey, in the inn, the Lord met him, and would have killed him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Immediately Sephora took a very sharp stone, and circumcised the foreskin of her son, and touched his feet, and said: A bloody spouse art thou to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he let him go after she had said: A bloody spouse art thou to me, because of the circumcision.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Aaron: Go into the desert to meet Moses. And he went forth to meet him in the mountain of God, and kissed him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses told Aaron all the words of the Lord, by which he had sent him, and the signs that he had commanded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they came together, and they assembled all the ancients of the children of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Aaron spoke all the words which the Lord had said to Moses: and he wrought the signs before the people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the people believed. And they heard that the Lord had visited the children of Israel, and that he had looked upon their affliction: and falling down they adored.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After these things, Moses and Aaron went in, and said to Pharao: Thus saith the Lord God of Israel: Let my people go, that they may sacrifice to me in the desert.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he answered: Who is the Lord, that I should hear his voice, and let Israel go? I know not the Lord, neither will I let Israel go.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they said: The God of the Hebrews hath called us, to go three days’ journey into the wilderness, and to sacrifice to the Lord our God; lest a pestilence or the sword fall upon us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The king of Egypt said to them: Why do you Moses and Aaron draw off the people from their works? Get you gone to your burdens.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao said: The people of the land are numerous; you see that the multitude is increased; how much more if you give them rest from their works?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore he commanded the same day the overseers of the works, and the task-masters of the people, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: You shall give straw no more to the people to make brick, as before; but let them go and gather straw.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And you shall lay upon them the task of bricks, which they did before; neither shall you diminish any thing thereof, for they are idle, and therefore they cry, saying: Let us go and sacrifice to our God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let them be oppressed with works, and let them fulfil them; that they may not regard lying words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the overseers of the works, and the taskmasters, went out and said to the people: Thus saith Pharao: I allow you no straw;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go, and gather it where you can find it; neither shall any thing of your work be diminished.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the people was scattered through all the land of Egypt to gather straw.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the overseers of the works pressed them, saying: Fulfil your work every day, as before ye were wont to do, when straw was given you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that were over the works of the children of Israel, were scourged by Pharao’s taskmasters, saying: Why have you not made up the task of bricks, both yesterday and to day, as before?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the officers of the children of Israel came, and cried out to Pharao, saying: Why dealest thou so with thy servants?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Straw is not given us, and bricks are required of us as before; behold we, thy servants, are beaten with whips, and thy people is unjustly dealt withal.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: You are idle, and therefore you say: Let us go and sacrifice to the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go therefore and work: straw shall not be given you, and you shall deliver the accustomed number of bricks.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the officers of the children of Israel saw that they were in evil case, because it was said to them: There shall not a whit be diminished of the bricks for every day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they met Moses and Aaron, who stood over against them as they came out from Pharao:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they said to them: The Lord see and judge, because you have, made our savour to stink before Pharao and his servants, and you have given him a sword, to kill us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses returned to the Lord, and said: Lord, why hast thou afflicted this people? wherefore hast thou sent me?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For since the time that I went in to Pharao to speak in thy name, he hath afflicted thy people: and thou hast not delivered them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Now thou shalt see what I will do to Pharao: for by a mighty hand shall he let them go, and with a strong hand shall he cast them out of his land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord spoke to Moses, saying: I am the Lord

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, by the name of God Almighty: and my name ADONAI I did not shew them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I made a covenant with them, to give them the land of Chanaan, the land of their pilgrimage wherein they were strangers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have heard the groaning of the children of Israel, wherewith the Egyptians have oppressed them: and I have remembered my covenant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore say to the children of Israel: I am the Lord who will bring you out from the work-prison of the Egyptians, and will deliver you from bondage: and redeem you with a high arm, and great judgments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I will take you to myself for my people, I will be your God: and you shall know that I am the Lord your God, who brought you out from the work-prison of the Egyptians:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And brought you into the land, concerning which I lifted up my hand to give it to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob: and I will give it you to possess: I am the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses told all this to the children of Israel: but they did not hearken to him, for anguish of spirit, and most painful work.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord spoke to Moses, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go in, and speak to Pharao king of Egypt, that he let the children of Israel go out of his land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moses answered before the Lord: Behold the children of Israel do not hearken to me: and how will Pharao hear me, especially as I am of uncircumcised lips?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord spoke to Moses and Aaron, and he gave them a charge unto the children of Israel, and unto Pharao the king of Egypt, that they should bring forth the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are the heads of their houses by their families. The sons of Ruben the firstborn of Israel: Henoch and Phallu, Hesron and Charmi.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are the kindreds of Ruben. The sons of Simeon, Jamuel and Jamin, and Ahod, and Jachin, and Soar, and Saul the son of a Chanaanitess: these are the families of Simeon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these are the names of the sons of Levi by their kindreds: Gerson, and Caath, and Merari. And the years of the life of Levi were a hundred and thirty-seven.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Gerson: Lobni and Semei, by their kindreds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Caath: Amram, and Isaar, and Hebron and Oziel. And the years of Caath’s life, were a hundred and thirty-three.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Merari: Moholi and Musi. These are the kindreds of Levi by their families.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Amram took to wife Jochabed his aunt by the father’s side: and she bore him Aaron and Moses. And the years of Amram’s life, were a hundred and thirty-seven.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons also of Isaar: Core, and Nepheg, and Zechri.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons also of Oziel: Mizael, and Elizaphan, and Sethri.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Aaron took to wife Elizabeth the daughter of Aminadab, sister of Nahason, who bore him Nadab, and Abiu, and Eleazar, and Ithamar.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons also of Core: Aser, and Elcana, and Abiasaph. These are the kindreds of the Corites.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Eleazar the son of Aaron took a wife of the daughters of Phutiel: and she bore him Phinees. These are the heads of the Levitical families by their kindreds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are Aaron and Moses, whom the Lord commanded to bring forth the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt by their companies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are they that speak to Pharao, king of Egypt, in order to bring out the children of Israel from Egypt: these are that Moses and Aaron,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the day when the Lord spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord spoke to Moses, saying: I am the Lord; speak thou to Pharao, king of Egypt, all that I say to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses said before the Lord: Lo I am of uncircumcised lips, how will Pharao hear me?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Behold, I have appointed thee the god of Pharao; and Aaron, thy brother, shall be thy prophet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt speak to him all that I command thee; and he shall speak to Pharao, that he let the children of Israel go out of his land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But I shall harden his heart, and shall multiply my signs and wonders in the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he will not hear you: and I will lay my hand upon Egypt, and will bring forth my army and my people, the children of Israel, out of the land of Egypt, by very great judgments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Egyptians shall know that I am the Lord, who have stretched forth my hand upon Egypt, and have brought forth the children of Israel out of the midst of them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses and Aaron did as the Lord had commanded; so did they.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses was eighty years old, and Aaron eighty-three, when they spoke to Pharao.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses and Aaron:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When Pharao shall say to you, Shew signs; thou shalt say to Aaron: Take thy rod, and cast it down before Pharao, and it shall be turned into a serpent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Moses and Aaron went in unto Pharao, and did as the Lord had commanded. And Aaron took the rod before Pharao and his servants, and it was turned into a serpent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao called the wise men and the magicians; and they also by Egyptian enchantments and certain secrets, did in like manner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they every one cast down their rods, and they were turned into serpents: but Aaron’s rod devoured their rods.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao’s heart was hardened, and he did not hearken to them, as the Lord had commanded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Pharao’s heart is hardened, he will not let the people go.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go to him in the morning, behold he will go out to the waters: and thou shalt stand to meet him on the bank of the river: and thou shalt take in thy hand the rod that was turned into a serpent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou shalt say to him: The Lord God of the Hebrews sent me to thee, saying: Let my people go to sacrifice to me in the desert: and hitherto thou wouldst not hear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thus therefore saith the Lord: In this thou shalt know that I am the Lord: behold I will strike with the rod, that is in my hand, the water of the river, and it shall be turned into blood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the fishes that are in the river, shall die, and the waters shall be corrupted, and the Egyptians shall be afflicted when they drink the water of the river.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord also said to Moses: Say to Aaron, Take thy rod; and stretch forth thy hand upon the waters of Egypt, and upon their rivers, and streams and pools, and all the ponds of waters, that they may be turned into blood: and let blood be in all the land of Egypt, both in vessels of wood and of stone.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses and Aaron did as the Lord had commanded: and lifting up the rod, he struck the water of the river before Pharao and his servants: and it was turned into blood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the fishes that were in the river died; and the river corrupted, and the Egyptians could not drink the water of the river, and there was blood in all the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the magicians of the Egyptians with their enchantments did in like manner; and Pharao’s heart was hardened, neither did he hear them, as the Lord had commanded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he turned himself away, and went into his house, neither did he set his heart to it this time also.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the Egyptians dug round about the river for water to drink; for they could not drink of the water of the river.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And seven days were fully ended, after that the Lord struck the river.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Go in to Pharao, and thou shalt say to him: Thus saith the Lord: Let my people go to sacrifice to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if thou wilt not let them go, behold I will strike all thy coasts with frogs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the river shall bring forth an abundance of frogs; which shall come up and enter into thy house, and thy bedchamber, and upon thy bed, and into the houses of thy servants, and to thy people, and into thy ovens, and into the remains of thy meats:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the frogs shall come in to thee, and to thy people, and to all thy servants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Say to Aaron: Stretch forth thy hand upon the streams, and upon the rivers and the pools, and bring forth frogs upon the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Aaron stretched forth his hand upon the waters of Egypt, and the frogs came up, and covered the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the magicians also, by their enchantments, did in like manner, and they brought forth frogs upon the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Pharao called Moses and Aaron, and said to them: Pray ye to the Lord to take away the frogs from me and from my people; and I will let the people go to sacrifice to the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses said to Pharao: Set me a time when I shall pray for thee, and for thy servants, and for thy people, that the frogs may be driven away from thee and from thy house, and from thy servants, and from thy people; and may remain only in the river.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he answered: To morrow. But he said: I will do according to thy word; that thou mayest know that there is none like to the Lord our God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the frogs shall depart from thee, and from thy house, and from thy servants, and from thy people; and shall remain only in the river.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses and Aaron went forth from Pharao: and Moses cried to the Lord for the promise, which he had made to Pharao concerning the frogs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord did according to the word of Moses: and the frogs died out of the houses, and out of the villages, and out of the fields:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they gathered them together into immense heaps, and the land was corrupted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao seeing that rest was given, hardened his own heart, and did not hear them, as the Lord had commanded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Say to Aaron: Stretch forth thy rod, and strike the dust of the earth; and may there be sciniphs in all the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they did so. And Aaron stretched forth his hand, holding the rod; and he struck the dust of the earth, and there came sciniphs on men and on beasts: all the dust of the earth was turned into sciniphs through all the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the magicians with their enchantments practised in like manner, to bring forth sciniphs, and they could not: and there were sciniphs as well on men as on beasts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the magicians said to Pharao: This is the finger of God. And Pharao’s heart was hardened, and he hearkened not unto them, as the Lord had commanded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord also said to Moses: Arise early, and stand before Pharao; for he will go forth to the waters: and thou shalt say to him: Thus saith the Lord: Let my people go to sacrifice to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if thou wilt not let them go, behold I will send in upon thee, and upon thy servants, and upon thy houses, all kind of flies: and the houses of the Egyptians shall be filled with flies of divers kinds, and the whole land wherein they shall be.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I will make the land of Gessen wherein my people is, wonderful in that day, so that flies shall not be there: and thou shalt know that I am the Lord in the midst of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I will put a division between my people and thy people: to morrow shall this sign be.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord did so. And there came a very grievous swarm of flies into the houses of Pharao and of his servants, and into all the land of Egypt: and the land was corrupted by this kind of flies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao called Moses and Aaron, and said to them: Go and sacrifice to your God in this land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses said: It cannot be so: for we shall sacrifice the abominations of the Egyptians to the Lord our God: now if we kill those things which the Egyptians worship, in their presence, they will stone us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We will go three days’ journey into the wilderness; and we will sacrifice to the Lord our God, as he hath commanded us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao said: I will let you go to sacrifice to the Lord your God in the wilderness, but go no farther: pray for me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses said: I will go out from thee, and will pray to the Lord: and the flies shall depart from Pharao, and from his servants, and from his people to morrow: but do not deceive any more, in not letting the people go to sacrifice to the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Moses went out from Pharao, and prayed to the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he did according to his word: and he took away the flies from Pharao, and from his servants, and from his people: there was not left so much as one.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao’s heart was hardened, so that neither this time would he let the people go.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Go in to Pharao, and speak to him: Thus saith the Lord God of the Hebrews: Let my people go to sacrifice to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if thou refuse, and withhold them still:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold my hand shall be upon thy fields; and a very grievous murrain upon thy horses, and asses, and camels, and oxen, and sheep.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord will make a wonderful difference between the possessions of Israel and the possessions of the Egyptians, that nothing at all shall die of those things that belong to the children of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord appointed a time, saying: To morrow will the Lord do this thing in the land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord therefore did this thing the next day: and all the beasts of the Egyptians died, but of the beasts of the children of Israel there died not one.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao sent to see; and there was not any thing dead of that which Israel possessed. And Pharao’s heart was hardened, and he did not let the people go.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses and Aaron: Take to you handfuls of ashes out of the chimney, and let Moses sprinkle it in the air in the presence of Pharao.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And be there dust upon all the land of Egypt: for there shall be boils and swelling blains both in men and beasts, in the whole land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they took ashes out of the chimney, and stood before Pharao, and Moses sprinkled it in the air; and there came boils with swelling blains in men and beasts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither could the magicians stand before Moses, for the boils that were upon them, and in all the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord hardened Pharao’s heart, and he hearkened not unto them, as the Lord had spoken to Moses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Arise in the morning, and stand before Pharao, and thou shalt say to him: Thus saith the Lord, the God of the Hebrews: Let my people go to sacrifice to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I will at this time send all my plagues upon thy heart, and upon thy servants, and upon thy people; that thou mayst know that there is none like me in all the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For now I will stretch out my hand to strike thee, and thy people, with pestilence, and thou shalt perish from the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And therefore have I raised thee, that I may shew my power in thee, and my name may be spoken of throughout all the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Dost thou yet hold back my people; and wilt thou not let them go?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold I will cause it to rain to morrow at this same hour, an exceeding great hail; such as hath not been in Egypt from the day that it was founded, until this present time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Send therefore now presently, and gather together thy cattle, and all that thou hast in the field; for men and beasts, and all things that shall be found abroad, and not gathered together out of the fields which the hail shall fall upon, shall die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that feared the word of the Lord among Pharao’s servants, made his servants and his cattle flee into houses:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he that regarded not the word of the Lord, left his servants, and his cattle in the fields.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Stretch forth thy hand towards heaven, that there may be hail in the whole land of Egypt upon men, and upon beasts, and upon every herb of the field in the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses stretched forth his rod towards heaven, and the Lord sent thunder and hail, and lightnings running along the ground: and the Lord rained hail upon the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the hail and fire mixt with it drove on together: and it was of so great bigness, as never before was seen in the whole land of Egypt since that nation was founded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the hail destroyed through all the land of Egypt all things that were in the fields, both man and beast: and the hail smote every herb of the field, and it broke every tree of the country.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Only in the land of Gessen, where the children of Israel were, the hail fell not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao sent and called Moses and Aaron, saying to them: I have sinned this time also, the Lord is just: I and my people, are wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Pray ye to the Lord that the thunderings of God and the hail may cease: that I may let you go, and that ye may stay here no longer.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moses said: As soon as I am gone out of the city, I will stretch forth my hands to the Lord, and the thunders shall cease, and the hail shall be no more: that thou mayst know that the earth is the Lord’s:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But I know that neither thou, nor thy servants do yet fear the Lord God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The flax therefore, and the barley were hurt, because the barley was green, and the flax was now bolled;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the wheat, and other winter corn were not hurt, because they were lateward.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Moses was gone from Pharao out of the city, he stretched forth his hands to the Lord: and the thunders and the hail ceased, neither did there drop any more rain upon the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao seeing that the rain, and the hail, and the thunders were ceased, increased his sin:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his heart was hardened, and the heart of his servants, and it was made exceeding hard: neither did he let the children of Israel go, as the Lord had commanded by the hand of Moses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Go in to Pharao; for I have hardened his heart, and the heart of his servants: that I may work these my signs in him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou mayst tell in the ears of thy sons, and of thy grandsons, how often I have plagued the Egyptians, and wrought my signs amongst them: and you may know that I am the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore Moses and Aaron went in to Pharao, and said to him: Thus saith the Lord God of the Hebrews: How long refusest thou to submit to me? let my people go, to sacrifice to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if thou resist, and wilt not let them go, behold I will bring in to-morrow the locusts into thy coasts;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To cover the face of the earth, that nothing thereof may appear, but that which the hail hath left may be eaten: for they shall feed upon all the trees that spring in the fields.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they shall fill thy houses, and the houses of thy servants, and of all the Egyptians: such a number as thy fathers have not seen, nor thy grandfathers, from the time they were first upon the earth, until this present day. And he turned himself away, and went forth from Pharao.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao’s servants said to him: How long shall we endure this scandal? Iet the men go to sacrifice to the Lord their God. Dost thou not see that Egypt is undone?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they called back Moses, and Aaron, to Pharao; and he said to them: Go, sacrifice to the Lord your God: who are they that shall go?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moses said: We will go with our young and old, with our sons and daughters, with our sheep and herds: for it is the solemnity of the Lord our God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao answered: So be the Lord with you, as I shall let you and your children go: who can doubt but that you intend some great evil?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It shall not be so, but go ye men only, and sacrifice to the Lord: for this yourselves also desired. And immediately they were cast out from Pharao’s presence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Stretch forth thy hand upon the land of Egypt unto the locust, that it come upon it, and devour every herb that is left after the hail.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses stretched forth his rod upon the land of Egypt: and the Lord brought a burning wind all that day, and night; and when it was morning, the burning wind raised the locusts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they came up over the whole land of Egypt; and rested in all the coasts of the Egyptians, innumerable, the like as had not been before that time, nor shall be hereafter.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they covered the whole face of the earth, wasting all things. And the grass of the earth was devoured, and what fruits soever were on the trees, which the hail had left; and there remained not any thing that was green on the trees, or in the herbs of the earth, in all Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore Pharao in haste called Moses and Aaron, and said to them: I have sinned against the Lord your God, and against you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But now forgive me my sin this time also, and pray to the Lord your God, that he take away from me this death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses going forth from the presence of Pharao, prayed to the Lord:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he made a very strong wind to blow from the west, and it took the locusts and cast them into the Red Sea: there remained not so much as one in all the coasts of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord hardened Pharao’s heart, neither did he let the children of Israel go.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Stretch out thy hand towards heaven: and may there be darkness upon the land of Egypt so thick that it may be felt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses stretched forth his hand towards heaven: and there came horrible darkness in all the land of Egypt for three days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: No man saw his brother, nor moved himself out of the place where he was: but wheresoever the children of Israel dwelt, there was light.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao called Moses and Aaron, and said to them: Go, sacrifice to the Lord: let your sheep only, and herds remain, let your children go with you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moses said: Thou shalt give us also sacrifices and burnt-offerings, to the Lord our God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All the flocks shall go with us; there shall not a hoof remain of them: for they are necessary for the service of the Lord our God: especially as we know not what must be offered, till we come to the very place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord hardened Pharao’s heart, and he would not let them go.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao said to Moses: Get thee from me, and beware thou see not my face any more: in what day soever thou shalt come in my sight, thou shalt die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moses answered: So shall it be as thou hast spoken, I will not see thy face anymore.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses: Yet one plague more will I bring upon Pharao and Egypt, and after that he shall let you go, and thrust you out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore thou shalt tell all the people, that every man ask of his friend, and every woman of her neighbour, vessels of silver and of gold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord will give favour to his people in the sight of the Egyptians. And Moses was a very great man in the land of Egypt, in the sight of Pharao’s servants, and of all the people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: Thus saith the Lord: At midnight I will enter into Egypt:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And every firstborn in the land of the Egyptians shall die, from the firstborn of Pharao who sitteth on his throne, even to the firstborn of the handmaid that is at the mill, and all the firstborn of beasts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there shall be a great cry in all the land of Egypt, such as neither hath been before, nor shall be hereafter.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But with all the children of Israel there shall not a dog make the least noise, from man even to beast; that you may know how wonderful a difference the Lord maketh between the Egyptians and Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all these thy servants shall come down to me, and shall worship me, saying: Go forth thou, and all the people that is under thee: after that we will go out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went out from Pharao exceeding angry. But the Lord said to Moses: Pharao will not hear you, that many signs may be done in the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses and Aaron did all the wonders that are written, before Pharao. And the Lord hardened Pharao’s heart, neither did he let the children of Israel go out of his land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses and Aaron in the land of Egypt:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This month shall be to you the beginning of months; it shall be the first in the months of the year.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Speak ye to the whole assembly of the children of Israel, and say to them: On the tenth day of this month let every man take a lamb by their families and houses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if the number be less than may suffice to eat the lamb, he shall take unto him his neighbour that joineth to his house, according to the number of souls which may be enough to eat the lamb.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it shall be a lamb without blemish, a male, of one year; according to which rite also you shall take a kid.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And you shall keep it until the fourteenth day of this month; and the whole multitude of the children of Israel shall sacrifice it in the evening.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they shall take of the blood thereof, and put it upon both the side posts, and on the upper door posts of the houses, wherein they shall eat it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they shall eat the flesh that night roasted at the fire, and unleavened bread with wild lettuce.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: You shall not eat thereof any thing raw, nor boiled in water, but only roasted at the fire; you shall eat the head with the feet and entrails thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither shall there remain any thing of it until morning. If there be any thing left, you shall burn it with fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thus you shall eat it: you shall gird your reins, and you shall have shoes on your feet, holding staves in your hands, and you shall eat in haste; for it is the Phase (that is the Passage) of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I will pass through the land of Egypt that night, and will kill every firstborn in the land of Egypt, both man and beast: and against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments; I am the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the blood shall be unto you for a sign in the houses where you shall be; and I shall see the blood, and shall pass over you; and the plague shall not be upon you to destroy you, when I shall strike the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And this day shall be for a memorial to you; and you shall keep it a feast to the Lord in your generations, with an everlasting observance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Seven days shall you eat unleavened bread: in the first day there shall be no leaven in your houses; whosoever shall eat any thing leavened, from the first day until the seventh day, that soul shall perish out of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The first day shall be holy and solemn, and the seventh day shall be kept with the like solemnity: you shall do no work in them, except those things that belong to eating.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And you shall observe the feast of the unleavened bread: for in this same day I will bring forth your army out of the land of Egypt, and you shall keep this day in your generations by a perpetual observance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The first month, the fourteenth day of the month, in the evening, you shall eat unleavened bread, until the one and twentieth day of the same month, in the evening.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Seven days there shall not be found any leaven in your houses: he that shall eat leavened bread, his soul shall perish out of the assembly of Israel, whether he be a stranger or born in the land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: You shall not eat any thing leavened: in all your habitations you shall eat unleavened bread.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses called all the ancients of the children of Israel, and said to them: Go take a lamb by your families, and sacrifice the Phase.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And dip a bunch of hyssop in the blood that is at the door, and sprinkle the transom of the door therewith, and both the door cheeks: let none of you go out of the door of his house till morning.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the Lord will pass through striking the Egyptians: and when he shall see the blood on the transom, and on both the posts, he will pass over the door of the house, and not suffer the destroyer to come into your houses and to hurt you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt keep this thing as a law for thee and thy children for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when you have entered into the land which the Lord will give you, as he hath promised, you shall observe these ceremonies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when your children shall say to you: What is the meaning of this service?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: You shall say to them: It is the victim of the passage of the Lord, when he passed over the houses of the children of Israel in Egypt, striking the Egyptians, and saving our houses. And the people bowing themselves, adored.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel going forth, did as the Lord had commanded Moses and Aaron.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it came to pass at midnight, the Lord slew every firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharao, who sat on his throne, unto the firstborn of the captive woman that was in the prison, and all the firstborn of cattle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao arose in the night, and all his servants, and all Egypt: and there arose a great cry in Egypt; for there was not a house wherein there lay not one dead.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao calling Moses and Aaron, in the night, said: Arise and go forth from among my people, you and the children of Israel: go, sacrifice to the Lord as you say.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Your sheep and herds take along with you, as you demanded, and departing bless me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Egyptians pressed the people to go forth out of the land speedily, saying: We shall all die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The people therefore took dough before it was leavened; and tying it in their cloaks, put it on their shoulders.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel did as Moses had commanded: and they asked of the Egyptians vessels of silver and gold, and very much raiment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord gave favour to the people in the sight of the Egyptians, so that they lent unto them: and they stripped the Egyptians.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel set forward from Ramesse to Socoth, being about six hundred thousand men on foot, beside children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And a mixed multitude, without number, went up also with them, sheep and herds, and beasts of divers kinds, exceeding many.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they baked the meal, which a little before they had brought out of Egypt in dough: and they made hearth cakes unleavened: for it could not be leavened, the Egyptians pressing them to depart, and not suffering them to make any stay; neither did they think of preparing any meat.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the abode of the children of Israel that they made in Egypt, was four hundred and thirty years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which being expired, the same day all the army of the Lord went forth out of the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This is the observable night of the Lord, when he brought them forth out of the land of Egypt: this night all the children of Israel must observe in their generations.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Moses and Aaron: This is the service of the Phase; no foreigner shall eat of it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But every bought servant shall be circumcised, and so shall eat.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The stranger and the hireling shall not eat thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In one house shall it be eaten, neither shall you carry forth of the flesh thereof out of the house, neither shall you break a bone thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All the assembly of the children of Israel shall keep it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if any stranger be willing to dwell among you, and to keep the Phase of the Lord, all his males shall first be circumcised, and then shall he celebrate it according to the manner: and he shall be as he that is born in the land: but if any man be uncircumcised, he shall not eat thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The same law shall be to him that is born in the land, and to the proselyte that sojourneth with you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the children of Israel did as the Lord had commanded Moses and Aaron.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the same day the Lord brought forth the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt by their companies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord spoke to Moses, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sanctify unto me every firstborn that openeth the womb among the children of Israel, as well of men as of beasts: for they are all mine.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses said to the people: Remember this day in which you came forth out of Egypt, and out of the house of bondage, for with a strong hand hath the Lord brought you forth out of this place: that you eat no leavened bread.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This day you go forth in the month of new corn.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the Lord shall have brought thee into the land of the Chanaanite, and the Hethite, and the Amorrhite, and the Hevite, and the Jebusite, which he swore to thy fathers that he would give thee, a land that floweth with milk and honey, thou shalt celebrate this manner of sacred rites in this month.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Seven days shalt thou eat unleavened bread: and on the seventh day shall be the solemnity of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Unleavened bread shall you eat seven days: there shall not be seen any thing leavened with thee, nor in all thy coasts.
